--- SOURCE 2: [PDF] Stakeholder Engagement and Collaboration in Health Policy ... ---
URL: https://www.seejph.com/index.php/seejph/article/download/491/374/566

SUMMARY:
ABSTRACT. Collaboration and involving stakeholders are very important for making sure that health policies are carried out well.

FULL CONTENT:
Mukesh Kumar, Vinati, Stakeholder Engagement and Collaboration in Health Policy Implementation: Lessons Learned. Submitted: 05-03-2024 Accepted: 26-04-2024 Posted: 24-05-2024, Vol. (XXIII) 104 | P a g e Stakeholder Engagement and Collaboration in Health Policy Implementation: Lessons Learned Dr Mukesh Kumar1, Vinati2 1Research Scholar, MDU Rohtak hooda.mukesh@gmail.com 2Assistant Professor NRS GC Rohtak vinati1190@gmail.com KEYWORDS Stakeholder engagement, Collaboration, Health policy, Implementatio n, Lessons learned ABSTRACT Collaboration and involving stakeholders are very important for making sure that health policies are carried out well. This is because it makes sure that different points of view are taken into account and that solutions are made to fit the needs of all stakeholders. This essay talks about how important it is for stakeholders to be involved and work together in putting health policies into action, focusing on important tactics and problems. Engaging stakeholders in a meaningful way is important to make sure that health policies reflect the wants and needs of the people they serve. Getting people involved, like patients, healthcare workers, neighborhood groups, and government bodies, can help make health policies that are more inclusive and last longer. For health policy development to work, everyone involved needs to work together to solve problems and reach shared goals. Finding and figuring out stakeholders, encouraging open lines of communication, and including stakeholders in decision-making processes are some of the most important things that can be done to get stakeholders involved and work together. Getting people involved early on and regularly during the policy-making and delivery process can help build trust and agreement, which can lead to better results. Even though working together and involving stakeholders can be helpful, there are some problems that need to be solved. Some of these are balancing the different interests of parties, making sure that all points of view are fairly represented, and handling problems that may come up. Also, not having enough resources or ability can make it harder to effectively involve and work with stakeholders. To deal with these problems, it's important to make clear rules and standards for involving stakeholders, set up ways to settle disagreements, and make sure that stakeholders have the right tools and support to take part. Getting parties to work together and form strong networks can also help get around problems and make efforts to implement health policies more effective. 1. Introduction Collaboration and involvement of stakeholders are important parts of putting health policies into action because they help make sure that policies are useful, include everyone, and last. Stakeholders, such as patients, healthcare professionals, community groups, and the government, must be involved in the policy-making process in order for it to meet the wants and desires of all areas. In order to solve Mukesh Kumar, Vinati, Stakeholder Engagement and Collaboration in Health Policy Implementation: Lessons Learned. Submitted: 05-03-2024 Accepted: 26-04-2024 Posted: 24-05-2024, Vol. (XXIII) 105 | P a g e difficult problems and reach shared goals in implementing health policies, all parties must work together.By affecting the quality, quantity, and ease of access to healthcare services, health policies have [1] a big impact on how healthy a community is. Implementing health policies successfully, however, requires more than just making good policies. It also needs active participation from all relevant parties to make sure that policies are carried out correctly and achieve their goals.Stakeholder participation in health policy application means that stakeholders are actively involved in all stages of decision-making, from making the policy to evaluating it. This involvement is important for many reasons. In the first place, stakeholders often have useful information and knowledge that can help make policies that are based on facts and meet the needs of communities. Second, involving stakeholders can help build trust and agreement around policies, which makes it more likely that they will be put into place and accepted by the community [2]. Lastly, including people who have a stake in the process of execution can help find and deal with possible problems or roadblocks early on, making sure that policies are carried out correctly.Collaboration between groups is also important for putting health policies into action because it lets them work together to reach shared goals. Collaboration can look like partnerships, teams, or networks, and it can include a lot of different groups, from neighborhood groups to non-governmental organizations (NGOs) to government agencies. Participants can use their combined skills, knowledge, and power to solve complicated health problems better when they work together than when they work alone [3], [4]. Even though working together and involving stakeholders is important, there are some problems that can make application less effective. Some of these problems are power differences between parties, competing goals, and a lack of resources. As an example, parties with more means or power may control the decision-making process, pushing other views to the sidelines. Similarly, parties with competing interests might find it hard to agree on policy choices, which could cause delays or policies that don't work [5]. Limited resources, such as money and people, can also make it hard for partners to play an active role in the application process.It is important to come up with methods for successful community involvement and teamwork in order to deal with these problems. This [6] means finding and figuring out the people who have a stake in the project, setting up clear lines of communication, and encouraging a culture of openness and inclusion. Building the ability of partners means giving them the tools and help they need to play a useful role in the execution process. Policymakers can make health policies more effective and improve people's health by solving these problems and encouraging stakeholders to work together and get involved. Overview of Research Objectives and Structure: This research paper aims to delve into the multifaceted aspects of stakeholder engagement and collaboration in health policy implementation, drawing on theoretical frameworks, empirical evidence, and real-world examples. The objectives of this paper are as follows: ● Examine Theoretical Frameworks: Explore the conceptual underpinnings of stakeholder engagement and collaboration in the context of health policy implementation, highlighting key theoretical models and frameworks Mukesh Kumar, Vinati, Stakeholder Engagement and Collaboration in Health Policy Implementation: Lessons Learned. Submitted: 05-03-2024 Accepted: 26-04-2024 Posted: 24-05-2024, Vol. (XXIII) 106 | P a g e guiding effective stakeholder involvement. ● Assess Significance of Stakeholder Engagement: Investigate the role of stakeholder engagement in shaping health policy development and implementation outcomes, emphasizing the impact of diverse stakeholder perspectives on policy effectiveness and equity. ● Identify Best Practices in Collaboration: Analyze case studies and examples of successful stakeholder collaboration models in health policy implementation, identifying best practices and strategies for fostering effective communication and cooperation among stakeholders. ● Address Challenges and Barriers: Identify common challenges and barriers to stakeholder engagement and collaboration in health policy implementation, and propose strategies for overcoming them to enhance policy effectiveness. ● Extract Lessons Learned: Synthesize key insights and lessons learned from experiences in stakeholder engagement and collaboration, offering practical implications for policymakers, healthcare professionals, and stakeholders involved in health policy implementation. ● Provide Recommendations: Offer practical recommendations for enhancing stakeholder engagement and collaboration in health policy implementation, tailored to address the unique needs and contexts of diverse stakeholders. Through a comprehensive examination of these objectives, this research paper aims to contribute to the growing body of knowledge on stakeholder engagement and collaboration in health policy implementation, providing valuable insights and guidance for stakeholders seeking to navigate the complexities of healthcare policymaking and implementation. 2. Theoretical Framework Understanding the ideas behind involving and working with stakeholders is important for figuring out what their roles are and how they affect the implementation of health policies. A number of academic models and theories have been created to help with the study and use of citizen involvement in healthcare and other fields. In this part, we'll talk about some of the most important academic views that affect how stakeholders are involved and how they work together to achieve health policies [7].This theory comes from management and says that companies are responsible to more than just their owners. They are also responsible to a larger group of people who have a stake in the company's activities and can affect those activities or be affected by them. When it comes to healthcare, stakeholder theory stresses how important it is to take into account the needs and points of view of many people, such as patients, healthcare workers, lawmakers, payers, and community members, when making and enforcing health policies. Stakeholder theory stresses how important it is to involve stakeholders in the policymaking process so that everyone benefits. This is because stakeholders play a big role in the success of an organization and in the well-being of society as a whole [8], [9]. Systems theory gives us a way to look at complicated systems and how their parts work together. In the setting of putting health policies Mukesh Kumar, Vinati, Stakeholder Engagement and Collaboration in Health Policy Implementation: Lessons Learned. Submitted: 05-03-2024 Accepted: 26-04-2024 Posted: 24-05-2024, Vol. (XXIII) 107 | P a g e into action, systems theory stresses how different people, groups, and things [10] that affect healthcare service and results are connected and affect each other. By looking at health systems as changing and adapting, systems theory stresses how important it is to involve partners at different levels (such as the person, the business, the community, and society) in order to solve systemic problems and encourage system-wide change. Systems theory helps us understand how complicated policy implementation is and why we need to work together and focus on systems. It does [11] this by looking at the bigger picture of health policies and the many people who have a stake in them. Figure 1: Overview of Theoretical Framework Frameworks for collaborative governance stress how important it is for parties to work together and form teams when making decisions and fixing problems. In healthcare, joint governance methods stress the importance of decision-making processes that include people from a wide range of experiences and fields. There are many groups involved in health problems that need to work together to solve them. These [12] groups include government bodies, healthcare workers, neighborhood groups, and private sector companies. Collaborative governance models help people agree on things and work together to reach common health policy execution goals and problems by encouraging trust, openness, and shared responsibility.This theory looks at how new tools, ideas, and ways of doing things spread over time in a society. When this theory is used to look at how health policies are put into action, it looks at how new policies are accepted, changed, and made bigger in different places and with different groups of people involved. Communication routes are an important part of the spread of ideas theory because they let people learn about new policies and decide to follow them. Stakeholder involvement is very important in this process because stakeholders both give and receive information and can change how health policies are adopted and put into place through their networks, relationships, and interactions [13]. Stakeholders and lawmakers can learn more about how to involve and work together with stakeholders in implementing health policies by using these academic viewpoints. These theoretical models give us a way to think about and examine how stakeholders connect with each other, find ways for people to work together, and deal with the problems that come up when policies are being made. In the parts that follow, we'll look at how these academic ideas show up in real-life cases of involving stakeholders and working together to achieve health policies. We'll focus on important lessons learned and the best ways to get good results. Mukesh Kumar, Vinati, Stakeholder Engagement and Collaboration in Health Policy Implementation: Lessons Learned. Submitted: 05-03-2024 Accepted: 26-04-2024 Posted: 24-05-2024, Vol. (XXIII) 108 | P a g e Table 1: Summary of related work Type of Stakeholder Policies Finding Application Government Agencies National Health Insurance Scheme Government agencies play a key role in the implementation of health policies, providing the regulatory framework and resources necessary for policy implementation. Government agencies are responsible for implementing and enforcing health policies, such as the National Health Insurance Scheme, which aims to provide universal health coverage for citizens. Healthcare Providers Electronic Health Records (EHRs) Healthcare providers are crucial stakeholders in health policy implementation, as they are responsible for implementing policies at the point of care. Healthcare providers implement policies related to the adoption of Electronic Health Records (EHRs), which improve the quality and efficiency of healthcare delivery by facilitating the electronic exchange of health information. Community Organizations Community Health Education Programs Community organizations play a vital role in health policy implementation by promoting community engagement and awareness. Community organizations implement health policies through community health education programs, which aim to raise awareness about health issues and promote healthy behaviors within communities. Patients Patient Bill of Rights Patients are important stakeholders in health policy implementation, as their experiences and perspectives inform policy development and implementation. Patients advocate for policies such as the Patient Bill of Rights, which protect their rights and ensure that they receive safe, quality healthcare services. Non-Governmental Organizations (NGOs) Vaccination Programs NGOs are key stakeholders in health policy implementation, often providing support and resources to government agencies and communities. NGOs implement health policies through vaccination programs, which aim to prevent the spread of infectious diseases and protect public health. Private Sector Public-Private Partnerships The private sector plays a significant role in health policy implementation through public-private partnerships, which leverage the resources and expertise of both sectors to improve health outcomes. The private sector implements health policies through public-private partnerships, such as the provision of healthcare services or the development of healthcare infrastructure. Academia Research and Policy Analysis Academia contributes to health policy implementation through research and policy analysis, providing evidence-based recommendations for policy development and implementation. Academia conducts research and policy analysis to inform health policy decisions, such as evaluating the impact of policies on health outcomes or identifying best practices for policy implementation. Mukesh Kumar, Vinati, Stakeholder Engagement and Collaboration in Health Policy Implementation: Lessons Learned. Submitted: 05-03-2024 Accepted: 26-04-2024 Posted: 24-05-2024, Vol. (XXIII) 109 | P a g e Media Health Promotion Campaigns The media plays a crucial role in health policy implementation by disseminating information and raising awareness about health issues. The media implements health policies through health promotion campaigns, which aim to educate the public about health risks and promote healthy behaviors. International Organizations Global Health Initiatives International organizations are important stakeholders in health policy implementation, often providing support and guidance to national governments. International organizations implement health policies through global health initiatives, such as the World Health Organization's (WHO) efforts to address global health challenges and promote health equity. Industry Stakeholders Food and Drug Safety Regulations Industry stakeholders are involved in health policy implementation through compliance with regulations and standards that ensure the safety and quality of healthcare products and services. Industry stakeholders implement health policies related to food and drug safety regulations, which protect consumers from unsafe or ineffective healthcare products. 3. Significance of Stakeholder Engagement Most people agree that involving stakeholders is an important part of putting health policies into action because they have a big impact on how policies are made, how they are put into action, and the results. This part will talk about why community involvement is important in putting health policies into action, focusing on how it affects the policies' usefulness, fairness, and long-term success. Including people [14] in the policy-making process helps make sure that policies take into account a range of needs, goals, and points of view. lawmakers can learn more about the problems and chances that different groups face in the real world by bringing people from different fields, such as healthcare workers, customers, community groups, and lawmakers. This method makes policies more relevant, possible, and acceptable, which increases their chances of working once they are put in place. Also, letting people have a say in how policies are made gives them a sense of ownership and commitment, which makes them more likely to follow through with their promises [15]. Getting stakeholders involved is a key part of promoting equality and fixing health results gaps. Policymakers can make sure that policies meet the needs of all groups, especially the most vulnerable or poor, by including overlooked or excluded groups in the governing process [16]. Inequalities, hurdles to access, and social factors of health that may have an effect on health results in certain areas can be found more easily when stakeholders are involved. By working together with different groups, lawmakers can create tailored programs and measures that deal with the underlying social, economic, and environmental issues that cause health inequalities. This [17] promotes health fairness and social justice. Including feedback and input from stakeholders in the policy-making process makes decisions more accountable and clear. Policymakers show they care about open communication, response, and inclusion by including people in policy talks. Mukesh Kumar, Vinati, Stakeholder Engagement and Collaboration in Health Policy Implementation: Lessons Learned. Submitted: 05-03-2024 Accepted: 26-04-2024 Posted: 24-05-2024, Vol. (XXIII) 110 | P a g e Figure 2: Workflow for Stakeholder Engagement Stakeholder involvement tools, like public meetings, expert groups, and community gatherings, give stakeholders a chance to say what they think, feel, and want. This makes lawmakers more responsible for what they do and decide. This way [18] of working with people builds trust and authority, which makes the deal between lawmakers and the people they serve stronger. Stakeholder involvement helps different groups of people work together and build their skills, which gives them the power to make important contributions to putting policies into action. Stakeholders can work together to solve tough health problems more effectively by sharing their knowledge, resources, and networks through partnerships. Stakeholder involvement encourages a mindset of working together and sharing responsibility. This leads to partnerships and group action, which creates new and long-lasting answers to health issues. Stakeholder involvement also helps people learn, share information, and improve their skills, which makes them better able to take part in future policy processes and projects.The stakeholder engagement is integral to the success of health policy implementation, facilitating collaboration, inclusivity, and accountability in decision-making processes [19]. By actively involving stakeholders, policymakers can harness the collective wisdom and resources of diverse stakeholders to develop policies that are responsive, equitable, and sustainable. In the following sections, we will explore examples of successful stakeholder engagement initiatives and best practices in health policy implementation, drawing on real-world experiences and lessons learned. 4. Best Practices in Stakeholder Collaboration For health policy development to go well, stakeholders must be able to work together effectively. Partners, open communication, and shared decision-making are some of the ways that stakeholders can work together to solve tough health problems and reach their joint objectives. In this part, we'll look at some of the best ways for stakeholders to work together, using cases from a range of policy and healthcare situations. Make sure that people with different backgrounds and points of view are involved in the process of working together. This could mean getting people from the government, healthcare providers, activist groups, neighborhood groups, schools, and businesses involved. Getting a lot of different opinions and points of view involved can help partners come up with new ways to solve tough health problems. Set up clear and open lines of Mukesh Kumar, Vinati, Stakeholder Engagement and Collaboration in Health Policy Implementation: Lessons Learned. Submitted: 05-03-2024 Accepted: 26-04-2024 Posted: 24-05-2024, Vol. (XXIII) 111 | P a g e contact so that partners can easily share information, give feedback, and work together. This could include regular classes, talks, groups, and online spaces where people can share resources, ideas, and news. When partners can talk to each other clearly, trust each other, and agree on what needs to be done, they can work together to reach their goals. Make sure that everyone involved in the teamwork agrees on and understands the shared goals and vision. Figure 3: Best Practices in Stakeholder Collaboration Make sure that everyone involved in the teamwork knows what it's for, how big it is, and what results you want it to have. By laying out a shared vision, parties can come together around a common goal and work together to make a big difference. Adopt ways of making decisions that allow everyone to have a say in important policy choices and methods for putting them into action. Encourage people to be involved, talk, and come to an agreement so that different points of view can be taken into account and used in the decision-making process. Policymakers can make sure that policies are responding to the needs and interests of those touched by them by including stakeholders in the decision-making process. Spend money on continued involvement, discussion, and teamwork to build trust and relationships among stakeholders. Encourage an atmosphere of respect, understanding, and mutuality so that everyone feels appreciated, heard, and accepted. Spend time and money on activities that help people get to know each other, like networking events, team-building activities, and partnership development projects, to improve ties and encourage a sense of community among stakeholders. Be able to change your plans and goals as needed to meet the needs of all stakeholders. Keep in mind that working together is an ongoing process that might need changes and adjustments along the way. Accept comments, lessons learned, and new chances to improve current teamwork tactics and make them work better over time. Stakeholders can better deal with uncertainty and problems if they stay flexible and quick to act. This makes sure that joint efforts are strong and last. Figure 4: Engagement Policy Development Commit to learning and evaluating all the time to figure out how well and how much impact community cooperation has. Create review tools, metrics, and signs to track progress toward shared results and goals. Ask partners and outside experts for feedback to find out what works, what doesn't, and where the teamwork process can be improved. Use the Mukesh Kumar, Vinati, Stakeholder Engagement and Collaboration in Health Policy Implementation: Lessons Learned. Submitted: 05-03-2024 Accepted: 26-04-2024 Posted: 24-05-2024, Vol. (XXIII) 112 | P a g e results of the review to help you plan, make decisions, and build your team's skills in the future. This will help you keep learning and getting better. By using these best practices, stakeholders can create an environment where people work together, form strong relationships, and bring about real changes in how health policies are put into action. Stakeholders can make their joint efforts more effective and long-lasting by following the ideals of inclusion, transparency, shared vision, and constant improvement. This will improve health results and move health fairness forward. In the next part, we'll look at some examples of successful stakeholder teamwork projects that helped put health policies into action. We'll also talk about what we learned and what we can use in the future. 5. Challenges and Barriers Even though working together with stakeholders to adopt health policies can be very helpful, there are some problems that can make it hard to do so. To get past problems and get the most out of group efforts, it's important to understand and deal with these issues. In this part, we'll talk about some of the most common problems and obstacles that come up when stakeholders work together, as well as ways to lessen their effects. 1. Diverse Stakeholder Priorities and Agendas: One of the hardest parts of working with other parties is getting their different goals, interests, and plans to work together. Stakeholders may have different goals or competing interests, which can make it hard to agree on policy choices or methods for putting them into action. Power differences between parties can also make things worse and make it harder for people to work together. ● Strategy: Help partners talk to each other in an open and honest way to find common ground, shared goals, and areas of interest for everyone. Set up clear ways to handle disagreements and make decisions, and make sure that everyone has a chance to participate in the joint process. To deal with different points of view and come to an agreement on important topics, encourage compromise and negotiation. 2. Limited Resources and Capacity: Working together with stakeholders takes a lot of time, money, and organizational strength to keep people engaged and involved in a useful way. However, a lot of players, especially community-based organizations and support groups, might not have the tools or knowledge they need to work together successfully. Stakeholders may also not be able to give joint efforts enough time and attention because of competing goals and limited resources within companies. Strategy: Help partners build their skills and provide professional help to make them better able to work together, especially those who don't have a lot of tools or knowledge. Encourage parties to work together and share resources in order to combine their knowledge, lower costs, and have the biggest effect possible. Make planning for longevity and allocating resources a top priority to make sure that joint projects will last. 3. Communication and Information Sharing Challenges: For stakeholder teamwork to work, people must be able to talk to each other clearly. However, communication problems like language hurdles, limited technology, and unequal knowledge can make it harder to work Mukesh Kumar, Vinati, Stakeholder Engagement and Collaboration in Health Policy Implementation: Lessons Learned. Submitted: 05-03-2024 Accepted: 26-04-2024 Posted: 24-05-2024, Vol. (XXIII) 113 | P a g e together. Stakeholders may have trouble getting up-to-date and useful information, which can lead to confusion, bad communication, and not agreeing on the goals and strategies for working together. ● Strategy: Set up clear routes and rules for contact to make it easier for parties to share information and work together. Use a variety of ways to communicate, such as face-to-face talks, virtual platforms, email updates, and social media, to reach people whose communication needs and tastes are different. Give people training and help on how to use effective tools and communication methods to make it easier for them to work together. 4. Organizational Culture and Resistance to Change: Organizational culture and resistance to change can make it hard for people to work together effectively in partner organizations. Some parties may not want to work together because they don't want to lose their independence, they are afraid of competition, or they don't believe that working together is useful. Bureaucratic processes and deeply rooted systems within companies may also make it harder to make quick decisions and respond to joint efforts. ● Strategy: Encourage a mindset of teamwork, new ideas, and constant improvement in partner groups by getting leadership support,

--- SOURCE 4: Building stakeholder trust: the heart of global health collaboration ---
URL: https://www.dhealth.consulting/global-health-consulting-insights/building-stakeholder-trust-the-heart-of-global-health-collaboration

SUMMARY:
Building trust isn't just about making projects run smoothly. It's about creating relationships that last and driving real impact in global health.

FULL CONTENT:
![D-Health Consulting](//images.squarespace-cdn.com/content/v1/619cf43bf57d5825346359c8/5a039423-667f-4c40-8052-8b4d8617ea34/DHC_fullcolor_web_transparentbg_primary_logo.png?format=1500w)
![D-Health Consulting](//images.squarespace-cdn.com/content/v1/619cf43bf57d5825346359c8/5a039423-667f-4c40-8052-8b4d8617ea34/DHC_fullcolor_web_transparentbg_primary_logo.png?format=1500w)

# Building stakeholder trust: the heart of global health collaboration

Global health challenges demand collective effort. Policymakers, clinicians, government officials, specialized organisations, NGOs, academia, patient advocacy groups and the private sector must align to create meaningful solutions. But with diverse priorities and perspectives at play, how do you build trust and foster collaboration?

Over the years, I’ve found that trust isn’t just built, it’s earned. Here are the key strategies I use to foster trust in global health collaborations:

**Start from a neutral perspective**

Approaching stakeholders without bias or hidden agendas is crucial. Transparency is the foundation of trust. I’m always clear about who is funding the initiative and clarify the steps I take to ensure balance and fairness. By demonstrating neutrality, I create an environment where stakeholders feel confident that their contributions will be valued equally.

**Understand the healthcare ecosystem**

Having a scientific background has been a game-changer. It enables me to bridge gaps between clinical, policy, and industry perspectives. Viewing healthcare as an interconnected system helps stakeholders see how their work fits into the broader initiative. This holistic understanding not only strengthens trust but also creates alignment across diverse stakeholders.

**Be genuinely interested in your stakeholders**

Knowing who your stakeholders are and why you chose them is essential. I take the time to understand their goals, motivations, and challenges. Building trust means showing that the initiative isn’t just about achieving your objectives but also about creating value for them. When stakeholders feel genuinely seen and heard, collaboration becomes much smoother.

**Propose clarity, but with flexibility**

Trust grows when stakeholders know what to expect. I always present a clear outline of goals, roles, and outcomes at the start of any collaboration. But it’s equally important to remain open to their ideas and suggestions. Incorporating their input not only improves the initiative but also shows that their perspectives truly matter.

**Follow through with consistency**

Delivering on commitments is one of the most effective ways to build trust. Whether it’s meeting deadlines, addressing concerns promptly, or showing genuine engagement over time, consistency reinforces reliability. Trust isn’t built overnight, it’s earned through consistent actions.

**Respect cultural and regional contexts**

In global health, cultural sensitivity is non-negotiable. Adapting approaches to the cultural and regional nuances of stakeholders ensures that initiatives resonate locally while staying aligned with global goals. Respecting these differences demonstrates empathy and understanding, which are crucial for building trust.

**Encourage mutual learning**

Collaboration is a two-way street. Every project is an opportunity for mutual learning. I value the unique expertise that stakeholders bring to the table and share insights from my experience bridging science, policy, and business. This exchange fosters a sense of partnership and trust.

**Acknowledge challenges openly**

Building trust isn’t always easy. Challenges like skepticism or resistance are common. I address these head-on with empathy and patience, creating an open dialogue that builds credibility even in the face of adversity.

**Conclusion**

Building trust isn’t just about making projects run smoothly. It’s about creating relationships that last and driving real impact in global health. By prioritising transparency, understanding, and genuine collaboration, we can create a foundation for partnerships that truly make a difference.

**Do you want to discuss more with me? Drop me an 💌 at** [**roberta.sarno@dhealth.consulting**](mailto:roberta.sarno@dhealth.consulting?)

SUMMARY:

Independent Study Project (ISP) Collection. 3758. https://digitalcollections.sit.edu/isp_collection/3758 This Unpublished Paper is brought to you for free and open access by the SIT Study Abroad at SIT Digital Collections. It has been accepted for inclusion in Independent Study Project (ISP) Collection by an authorized administrator of SIT Digital Collections. For more information, please contact digitalcollections@sit.edu. From Collaboration to Continuity: Exploring Stakeholder Engagement for Sustainable Global Health Partnerships By Annabelle Falconer Spring 2024 SIT Switzerland: Global Health and Development Policy Alexander Lambert Tulane University Public Health and International Development 1 Abstract Global health partnerships have been identified as crucial to achieving the Sustainable Development Goals and targets related to health and wellbeing. Specifically, the Global Fund, UNAIDS Joint Programme, and the World Food Programme are examples of some of the relevant partners currently engaged in this work, filling roles in global health financing, governance, and delivery. Current literature has identified that the effective engagement of stakeholders in partnerships can be crucial in effective project implementation and that global health partnerships during the era of the Millennium Development Goals did impact health outcomes. This research aims to investigate the current strategies for stakeholder engagement as well as identify the techniques used by partners to implement strategies or coordination with stakeholders that are sustainable in the long term. Analysis of organizational reports and interviews with experts identified that global health partners are making efforts to include local stakeholder and country perspectives and needs in their work but still face barriers in bridging the gap between bringing these voices to the table and ultimately giving them the space to be heard and centered. Additionally, various sustainability initiatives have been proposed by partners to increase the longevity and success of their work. Accountability and coordinating mechanisms for ensuring that all actors involved in these initiatives know their role is still a point of weakness. Lack of adequate funding to ensure the success of these long-term projects is an additional barrier. Currently, not enough data is available to fully identify the effectiveness of these sustainability strategies as measured in health outcomes. Ultimately, this research provides a checkpoint into the current state of global health partnerships as they relate to stakeholder involvement and long-term strength and effectiveness. 2 Acknowledgments Foremost, I would like to express my gratitude and appreciation to my professors at Tulane University, who have given me the skills and knowledge that built the foundation for my learning while participating in the School for International Training’s Global Health and Development Policy Program. I would also like to thank the academic support and advising staff of SIT, specifically Dr. Elisabeth Meur, for her help in filtering my many ideas into something tackleable for this project. Additionally, I would like to thank Ms. Francoise Flourens for her help in connecting to contacts for this research. I would like to give a special token of my appreciation to my peers in this program who provided motivational and intellectual support throughout the process of my research. Finally, I would like to thank my parents for assisting me in being able to access this experience and for their motivational and emotional support throughout the entire process. 3 Table of Contents 243 Techniques and Implications of Strategies in Fostering Sustainable Impact and Change ......................................................................................................................................... 255 Conclusion ................................................................................................................................. 287 Abbreviations ............................................................................................................................ 300 Citations ..................................................................................................................................... 311 4 Introduction The Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs), launched in 2015 off the back of the Millenium Development Goals (MDGs), were created in response to the success, challenges, and lessons learned from the MDGs and as commitment mechanisms and roadmaps for governments, public and private organizations and members of civil society for development around the world.1 These goals included various targets aimed toward “ensuring healthy lives and promoting well-being for all at all ages” and were related to the reduction of poverty, hunger, and disease.2 Within this realm, global health partnerships (GHPs) have been identified as critical partners in the achievement of these goals. GHPs can be understood as “a multiyear working relationship between two or more organizations from different countries.” 3 These partnerships include the interaction between various actors and stakeholders who are a part of the global health realm. For this research, the strategies undertaken by three key actors in the global health field will be analyzed. These specific actors are identified as the Global Fund to Fight AIDS, Tuberculosis and Malaria (GF), The World Food Programme (WFP), and the UNAIDS Joint Programme. The Global Fund strictly provides financial support for programs targeting their three focus diseases: AIDS, TB, and Malaria. The organization acts as a funding body that receives money pledged from governments, the private sector, and foundations and then distributes those funds in a coordinated manner.4 They work in partnership to review 1 Jeffrey D. Sachs, “From Millennium Development Goals to Sustainable Development Goals,” The Lancet 379, no. 9832 (June 9, 2012): 2206–11, https://doi.org/10.1016/S0140-6736(12)60685-0. 2 World Health Organization, “Sustainable Development Goals,” 2023, https://www.who.int/data/gho/data/themes/sustainable-development-goals. 3 Simone H. Schriger et al., “Hierarchy of Qualities in Global Health Partnerships: A Path towards Equity and Sustainability,” BMJ Global Health 6, no. 12 (December 1, 2021): e007132, https://doi.org/10.1136/bmjgh-2021-007132. 4 The Global Fund, “About the Global Fund,” 2024, https://www.theglobalfund.org/en/about-the-global-fund/. 5 applications and provide the financing needed for proposed projects but don’t operate directly on the ground outside of their Geneva headquarters.5 The WFP and UNAIDS are both organizations that exist under the umbrella of the United Nations (UN) system and are committed to addressing issues and providing support related to health and wellbeing; however, their focus and approach in the context of GHPs differ. UNAIDS is cosponsored by 11 UN organizations, all of which have connections to the fight against HIV/AIDS through their multifaceted approach to reducing deaths, barriers to care, and discrimination.6 Working within the global health sphere since 1996, UNAIDS primarily functions in the governing sphere, providing a strategic direction for the knowledge, policy, coordination, and technical support needed to connect governments, the private sector, and communities to better health outcomes.7 UNAIDS also provides funding to projects but at a significantly smaller scale than other donors in the field, accounting for less than 1% of all funding available for HIV in low and middle-income countries (LMICs).8 The World Food Programme is the largest humanitarian organization globally. It works directly in resource delivery for conflict and disaster zones, providing food to areas at high risk of undernutrition.9 Additionally, the WFP acts as a key global health partner in long-term development projects targeted toward strengthening food systems and building resilient communities and livelihoods.10 Funding for the program is made up entirely of voluntary donations, with the money raised going directly toward WFP operations and projects.11 5 The Global Fund, “About the Global Fund.” 6 UN AIDS, “About UNAIDS,” 2024, https://www.unaids.org/en/whoweare/about. 7 UN AIDS, “About UNAIDS.” 8 UNAIDS Press Centre, “UNAIDS Calls for Greater and Sustained Funding Commitments to Support Its Work in Leading Global Efforts to End AIDS by 2030.” 9 World Food Programme, “Who We Are,” 2024, https://www.wfp.org/who-we-are. 10 World Food Programme, “Who We Are.” 11 World Food Programme, “Who We Are.” 6 In the aftermath of the Millennium Development Goals, various studies were conducted to analyze the impact of GHPs on the health outcomes of individual stakeholders and health systems. This research aims to provide a checkpoint for the strategies used by three current actors in the global health realm. This checkpoint is conducted to ultimately answer the question of how these actors engage local stakeholders in their projects and partnerships during the era of SDGs, as well as provide an analysis of the techniques of these strategies and additional implemented plans in fostering sustainable and lasting impact and change, beyond the course of their involvement. Literature Review Defining the Actors and Stakeholders The global health system has evolved significantly over the last few decades to become what it is today. This evolution has come as the goals of the global health system have equally changed. As a result, it is necessary to redefine the current actors and stakeholders in this sphere and the general roles that they play in the field. A 2018 study used systematic mapping to identify the current actors in the global health network, defining this group as “individuals or organizations that operate transnationally with a primary intent to improve health.” 12 Examples of those meeting this definition included but were not limited to: national governments, intergovernmental organizations, multilateral development banks, public-private partnerships, 12 Steven J. Hoffman and Clarke B. Cole, “Defining the Global Health System and Systematically Mapping Its Network of Actors,” Globalization and Health 14, no. 1 (April 17, 2018): 38, https://doi.org/10.1186/s12992-018-0340-2. 7 philanthropic organizations, and academic institutions. These actors operated in both the public and private spheres to engage in work with the primary intent of improving health.13 Further, this mapping arranges actors into three realms within the fields of global health: governance, financing, and delivery, noting that actors can operate in or engage in multiple of these fields simultaneously with the work they do.14 This study primarily outlines the high-level actors involved in global health systems, most of which are headquartered in and operate out of high-income countries, including Switzerland and the United States.15 However, this research is not an exhaustive list of all actors or stakeholders involved in global health work. An additional 2022 study on this topic supplements this list, adding in the essential roles that the countries and communities that are often recipients of these initiatives play in the broader partnerships. These partners are primarily known as stakeholders.16 On-the-ground stakeholders often include local governments, ministries of health, community leaders, and the “end users,” who can be understood as the specific community or country population directly aimed to benefit from the global health initiative.17 Outlining Stakeholder and Actor Partnerships Once the relevant actors and stakeholders involved in the global health system are defined, it is necessary to investigate the existing research on the ways in which these involved partners interact within the sphere of initiative implementation. High-level global health actors can operate in multiple realms to contribute to the goal of improving health-related outcomes; 13 Hoffman and Cole, “Defining the Global Health System and Systematically Mapping Its Network of Actors.” 14 Hoffman and Cole, “Defining the Global Health System and Systematically Mapping Its Network of Actors.” 15 Hoffman and Cole, “Defining the Global Health System and Systematically Mapping Its Network of Actors.” 16 Julie Jacobson and Alan Brooks, “Reflections on ‘Orchestrating for Impact’: Harmonizing across Stakeholders to Accelerate Global Health Gains,” The American Journal of Tropical Medicine and Hygiene 106, no. 5 Suppl (May 2022): 67–69, https://doi.org/10.4269/ajtmh.21-1101. 17 Jacobson and Brooks, “Reflections on ‘Orchestrating for Impact.’” 8 this involvement is often referred to in the literature as international aid. Existing research on the current application of international aid globally primarily points to its use in four main realms as it engages in partnerships to produce necessary health goods, manage cross-border concerns, mobilize global solidarity and assistance as well as “support and catalyze” the efforts to improve health systems.18 All of these goals involve understanding the needs of the stakeholders whom they aim to serve and, as a result, some form of coordination to be deemed successful.19 Additional research conducted to identify the characteristics of sound partnerships also emphasizes the necessity of defining shared goals and assigning roles and expectations to each group based on a shared understanding of the initiative's outcome. A central point of organization and delegation is paramount for the productivity and coherence of plan implementation.20 A crucial part of this step is engaging the diverse perspectives of stakeholder assets, understanding what they need, what they can bring to the table, their incentives, and integrating their knowledge throughout the process.21 Throughout the process, ongoing communication and evaluation between all partners ensures efficient work that achieves the goals and interests of both the actors and stakeholders.22 Over time, effective partnerships continue to integrate communication and feedback into future initiatives, ensuring that they are attending to the changing perspectives of the stakeholders as environments, leadership, and needs change.23 18 Paul Nelson, “Global Development Goals and Global Health: The Power and Problems of International Aid,” The Brown Journal of World Affairs 21, no. 2 (2015): 149–62. 19 Nelson, “Global Development Goals and Global Health.” 20 Jacobson and Brooks, “Reflections on ‘Orchestrating for Impact.’” 21 Jacobson and Brooks, “Reflections on ‘Orchestrating for Impact.’” 22 Jacobson and Brooks, “Reflections on ‘Orchestrating for Impact.’” 23 Jacobson and Brooks, “Reflections on ‘Orchestrating for Impact.’” 9 Recent Historical Impacts of Stakeholder and Actor Partnerships Within the last 30 years, various research studies have been conducted on the impacts of these partnerships and their functioning on overall health outcomes in the places where project implementation and interventions have taken place. Research recently published on behalf of the Organisation for Economic Cooperation and Development has established that development assistance for health (DAH), primarily in the form of monetary international aid provided by global health actors, has increased significantly to the point that it has become the growing share of health budgets in LMICs.24 The impacts, according to this research, are mixed. From a health outcome standpoint, it is clear that there has been an improvement since 1990--this indeed can be partially credited to DAH, with one of the most significant indicators being the reduction in child mortality, which was assisted by DAH-financed technologies and research.25 However, in the same study, the role of DAH in creating continued dependencies was highlighted. This research points out that the overreliance on DAH results in a reduction of specific health commitments from the receiving country's side in both budgetary allowances as well as internal investment in health systems.26 In addition, the historical reliance on DAH reduces opportunities for the Global South to develop its own network of suppliers, institutions, and local financing. Finally, this research argues that in recent history, DAH has created power asymmetries between donors and recipients through the dependencies created in the provision of aid.27 Additional research investigating the role of development assistance during this same time frame largely corroborates the results of the initial study. A 2015 study on the topic 24 Olusoji Adeyi, “In Focus: Transitioning out of Aid Dependency in Health,” in Development Co-Operation Report 2023: Debating the Aid System (Organisation for Economic Cooperation and Development, 2024). 25 Olusoji Adeyi, “In Focus: Transitioning out of Aid Dependency in Health.” 26 Olusoji Adeyi, “In Focus: Transitioning out of Aid Dependency in Health.” 27 Olusoji Adeyi, “In Focus: Transitioning out of Aid Dependency in Health.” 10 specifically analyzed the impacts through the lens of the health-related Millennium Development Goals, which spanned from 2000 to 2015. In this investigation, the authors found that development assistance did contribute to the improvement of critical indicators, including the previously reported reduction in child mortality, as well as a reduction in maternal mortality, HIV/AIDs, malaria, and the proportion of people without access to safe water and sanitation.28 During this period, development assistance targeting these issues increased dramatically, and improvements were seen in the measurement of the overall prevalence of these health concerns.29 However, this study also points out additional concerns about the unintended negative impacts of this form of involvement from global health actors. In the shift toward disease-specific intervention and the reduction in overall prevalence, support for closing the gaps in outcomes for marginalized populations was largely forgotten. In addition, due to the nature of the funding for specific vertically integrated initiatives, goals and efforts for the long-term strengthening of national health systems were largely ignored, leaving the effectiveness of the sustainability of these initiatives in question.30 Implications of this Review on New Research From this review of the existing research on the roles of the actors and stakeholders and their respective partnerships in implementing initiatives within the Global Health system, it is clear that this is a current and relevant sector for research. While there is an existing understanding of the ideal nature of what these partnerships look like, who's involved, and their impacts from a historical standpoint, there is a place for additional research on the more current 28 Nelson, “Global Development Goals and Global Health.” 29 Nelson, “Global Development Goals and Global Health.” 30 Nelson, “Global Development Goals and Global Health.” 11 state of these partnerships. Specifically, the role of actors and strategies for effective stakeholder engagement in the new era of sustainable development goals (SDGs). An evaluation of these current actions is necessary to understand how this subject has evolved over time and how it exists in the present global health sphere. Research Methods and Ethics Design and Approach Qualitative multi-case study research using both primary and secondary sources was undertaken. Primary sources were gathered from semi-structured formal and informal interviews. Secondary sources were collected through a search of existing literature using keywords that included but were not limited to: “Global Health Partnerships, Global Health Financing, Global Health Governance, Stakeholders, Sustainability, Health Systems, Community Ownership, and Health Systems strengthening” on databases including JSTOR and Google Scholar. Secondary sources also included grey literature, press releases, and self-published action reports from chosen global health organizations. This approach was taken to best incorporate the expertise of professionals in the field with the plethora of existing information published by the selected organizations, which exists due to their commitment to transparency in their operations. Recruitment This research based contact and outreach to potential subjects on two primary criteria: Organizational relevance to the research question and proximity to the research location in Geneva, Switzerland. Based on these criteria, over 25 organizations were identified. The most significant barrier faced in this process was that of nonresponse. Of those contacted, only three 12 responded for interviews. Additionally, the post-pandemic professional work setting meant that although the interviewees were based in Geneva, most continued to operate in a remote work format. Due to the nature of their schedule and this limitation, the interviews for this research were conducted in a virtual format, like Microsoft Teams. Additional limitations of this research include the small sample size and the lack of diverse perspectives on the topic for each organization. Insights on how global health organizations integrate the perspectives of stakeholders in their research are entirely based on what is reported on by the organizations themselves and the experts interviewed, as community stakeholders themselves were not available interview subjects. Interviews and Ethics This research interviewed three professionals who worked for the selected global health organizations based in Geneva, Switzerland. Formal interviews were conducted in a semi-structured virtual meeting format. The participants were informed about the nature of the research and chose to participate voluntarily, and oral consent to use their perspectives was discussed. Requests for anonymity were respected if required. Additionally, if information obtained in the interview was requested to not be directly cited, corroborating findings will be included instead through other official sources published by the organization. Analysis Global Fund Identifying SDGs and Stakeholders Within the global health partnership sphere, the Global Fund has played the role of a financing agent since 2001, committed to upholding its mandate to support the fight against 13 AIDS, TB, and Malaria. In the strides that it has made in recent decades, it has also expanded its approach to go the extra mile to close the gap by tackling the underlying social, environmental, and economic factors that keep these diseases in communities. In doing so, they have more recently expanded efforts to address these factors of climate change, gender, health system strengthening, and pandemic preparedness.31 As a result, the Global Funds approach addresses both direct and indirect targets of the SDGs. This primarily includes Target 3.3 of Goal 3, ending the epidemics of AIDS, TB, and Malaria, but their expanded work has also impacted gender equity and education.32 Stakeholders and actors involved with the Global Fund include academic institutions, civil society, faith organizations, organizations in the private and public sectors, governments, and multi or bilateral agencies. Additionally, primary stakeholders that interact with the involvement of the GF include individuals and communities directly impacted or affected by their target diseases.33 Strategies for Stakeholder Engagement At the core of its structure, the Global Fund aims to incorporate all relevant stakeholders within the proposals for which they ultimately grant funding. The web of Global Fund stakeholders and actors is complex. Country Coordinating Mechanisms (CCMs) are at the heart of the process. These national committees comprise all relevant representatives of disease response, specific to that of the country, many of whom are the stakeholders previously outlined. One uniform requirement across each CCM board is the representation of people directly living with the targeted disease, HIV/AIDS, TB, or Malaria.34 This country-specific and country-led 31 Nicholas Furtado, interview by author, Online Format, April 16, 2024. 32 World Health Organization, “Sustainable Development Goals,” 33 Introduction to the Global Fund and CCMs, 2012, https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=qOPU9f6MM0E. 34 The Global Fund Board, “Country Coordinating Mechanism Policy,” 2018, https://www.theglobalfund.org/media/7421/ccm_countrycoordinatingmechanism_policy_en.pdf. 14 approach is arranged so that those who know best about the needs of the area can create proposals and pathways for the Global Fund to provide financing. An interview with an expert on the topic clarified, though, that while the CCM models are set up to do their best to include the opinions and perspectives of direct stakeholders, the applications of these voices will vary and that, ultimately, there is no guarantee about what makes it to the final proposal.35 More clearly, power differences between those involved may impact what is finally included in the proposal due to the multitude of reshaping and revision that goes on within the process. Ultimately, knowledge of three primary considerations, the technicality of the project, politics, and finances, influence what voices are most influential in the creation of the final proposal presented to the Global Fund.36 Additionally, a report on the stakeholders' opinions found that they felt that CCMs were dominated by governments and that more power-sharing was needed.37 The acknowledgment of this power imbalance highlights a point of challenge for the organization as it continues to evolve in the changing climate of strategies for disease prevention and control. When proposals for the GF to finance are ultimately approved, the CMM chooses the most capable organizations to run the projects. These organizations, known as principal recipients (PR), receive the money directly from the GF.38 Ultimately, the GF runs on a performance-based model, meaning projects th

enriched_points.expanded_content: Governments must collaborate to align health policies, share resources, and address global health challenges effectively, ensuring equitable access to healthcare for all populations.
Health organizations play a vital role in implementing health policies. Engaging them fosters a comprehensive approach to address community-specific health needs and improves overall health outcomes.
Community involvement ensures that health policies reflect local needs, enhancing acceptance and effectiveness. Engaging communities empowers them and fosters ownership of health initiatives.
